message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY REL-1.

FINDING REL-1 (low): SIGTERM handler swallows the signal when a prior C-installed handler makes getsignal() return None
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py _install_flush_on_exit._handler
Claim: The pass-8 K-REL-1 fix handles prior==SIG_DFL (re-deliver) and callable prior (chain). But signal.getsignal() returns None when the previous handler was installed from NON-Python (C) code. None is neither callable nor == SIG_DFL, so the handler flushes and RETURNS without terminating or chaining — swallowing SIGTERM again in that case. VERIFY: is None a reachable getsignal() return, and does the handler's elif (prior==SIG_DFL) miss it, falling through to no termination? Assess realism (a C-installed SIGTERM handler before bootstrap_tracing).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code and testing the behavior.

content.content:
1	"""Self-hosted tracing — OpenTelemetry → a local collector (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Ported and generalized from the prior platform's Phoenix/OTel bootstrap. Everything is
4	on-box: spans export over OTLP to a loopback-bound collector; nothing leaves the machine.
5	OpenTelemetry and the Phoenix collector are optional (the ``observability`` extra) and
6	imported lazily, so this module loads — and the engine runs untraced — with neither present.
7	
8	The reasoning seam already emits spans through the global OTel tracer provider; calling
9	:func:`bootstrap_tracing` once at startup wires that global provider at a local collector so
10	those spans land somewhere. Without the extra (or with ``ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`` set),
11	tracing degrades to a silent no-op.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	import atexit
17	import logging
18	import os
19	import signal
20	import threading
21	from contextlib import contextmanager
22	from typing import Any, Iterator, Mapping, Protocol
23	
24	from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_host
25	
26	_log = logging.getLogger("assay_engine.observability")
27	
28	# Local collector endpoint (loopback-enforced).
29	TRACING_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_HOST", "localhost")
30	
31	
32	def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
33	    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).
34	
35	    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
36	    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
37	    """
38	    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
39	    try:
40	        return int(raw)
41	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
42	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
43	
44	
45	TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
46	PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")
47	
48	_provider: Any = None
49	# Guards the _provider check-then-set so concurrent bootstrap_tracing() calls don't race.
50	_provider_lock = threading.Lock()
51	
52	
53	def _disabled() -> bool:
54	    return os.environ.get("ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
55	
56	
57	def tracing_base_url() -> str:
58	    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
59	    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"
60	
61	
62	def tracing_endpoint() -> str:
63	    """OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint at the local collector (loopback-enforced)."""
64	    return f"{tracing_base_url()}/v1/traces"
65	
66	
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None
110	
111	
112	def _warn_if_not_global(provider: Any) -> None:
113	    """If another provider already won the global slot, our manual spans would vanish (#O6)."""
114	    try:
115	        from opentelemetry import trace
116	
117	        if trace.get_tracer_provider() is not provider:
118	            _log.warning(
119	                "assay tracing: another OpenTelemetry provider holds the global slot; "
120	                "manually-emitted spans may not reach the local collector"
121	            )
122	    except Exception:
123	        pass
124	
125	
126	def _instrument_langchain() -> None:
127	    """Capture LangChain auto-spans (the bulk reasoning tier uses LangChain) — best-effort (#O5)."""
128	    try:
129	        from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor
130	
131	        LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(skip_dep_check=True)
132	    except Exception:
133	        pass
134	
135	
136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                # Preserve the ORIGINAL disposition (#K-REL-1). If a Python handler pre-existed,
154	                # chain to it. Otherwise prior is SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN — both NON-callable — and simply
155	                # returning here would SWALLOW the signal: we replaced SIGTERM's default
156	                # terminate-the-process action with a flush-and-return, so `docker stop` / `kill` /
157	                # k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation. Re-establish and re-deliver the default
158	                # action so the process still dies on SIGTERM (SIG_IGN stays ignored).
159	                if callable(prior):
160	                    prior(signum, frame)
161	                elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
162	                    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
163	                    os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)  # re-deliver → default terminate action runs
164	
165	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
166	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
167	            pass
168	
169	
170	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
171	    try:
172	        fn()
173	    except Exception:
174	        pass
175	
176	
177	@contextmanager
178	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:
179	    """Stamp ``run_id`` onto every span emitted inside the block (cross-store correlation).
180	
181	    A no-op when ``run_id`` is None or OpenTelemetry is absent.
182	    """
183	    if run_id is None:
184	        yield
185	        return
186	    try:
187	        from opentelemetry import baggage, context
188	    except ImportError:
189	        yield
190	        return
191	    token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("assay.run_id", run_id))
192	    try:
193	        yield
194	    finally:
195	        context.detach(token)
196	
197	
198	# OpenInference requires every span carry a span kind so Phoenix classifies it (CHAIN/LLM/
199	# AGENT/TOOL); a plain OTel span with no kind renders as UNKNOWN in the Phoenix UI. We set the
200	# raw semantic-convention attribute directly (no openinference import needed for a manual span).
201	_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND = "openinference.span.kind"
202	
203	
204	# Structural Protocol only — adapter/seam validation is behavior-based, not isinstance (#148).
205	class Tracer(Protocol):
206	    @contextmanager
207	    def span(
208	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
209	    ) -> Iterator[None]: ...
210	
211	
212	class OtelTracer:
213	    """A tracer that emits OpenTelemetry spans via the global provider (no-op if OTel absent).
214	
215	    Use this where a component wants explicit spans rather than relying on auto-instrumentation;
216	    it honors whatever provider :func:`bootstrap_tracing` registered. Each span is stamped with
217	    its OpenInference ``kind`` so Phoenix classifies it (default ``CHAIN``).
218	    """
219	
220	    @contextmanager
221	    def span(
222	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
223	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
224	        try:
225	            from opentelemetry import trace
226	        except ImportError:
227	            yield
228	            return
229	        tracer = trace.get_tracer("assay_engine")
230	        # start_as_current_span defaults record_exception=True and set_status_on_exception=True,
231	        # so an exception escaping the body is automatically recorded as a span event and the span
232	        # status is set to ERROR with the exception text (verified in test_observability) — #F-005
233	        # was a false positive; no manual error-recording is needed here.
234	        with tracer.start_as_current_span(name) as sp:
235	            sp.set_attribute(_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, kind)
236	            for k, v in (attributes or {}).items():
237	                sp.set_attribute(k, v)
238	            yield
239	
240	
241	class UnconfiguredTracer:
242	    """Fail-loud placeholder for contexts that must not silently drop spans."""
243	
244	    @contextmanager
245	    def span(
246	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
247	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
248	        raise NotImplementedError(
249	            "no tracer configured — call bootstrap_tracing() and use OtelTracer, or supply a "
250	            "test double"
251	        )
252	        yield  # pragma: no cover - keeps this a generator/contextmanager
253

content.text:
The finding is precisely identifiable. The handler at lines 159-163 has two branches: `callable(prior)` and `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL`. The finding claims that `signal.getsignal()` can return `None` (when the prior handler was installed from C/non-Python code), and `None` is neither callable nor `== SIG_DFL`, so the handler would fall through and just return after flushing — swallowing the signal.

Let me verify the documented behavior of `getsignal` and reproduce.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import signal, inspect
print('getsignal doc:')
print(inspect.getdoc(signal.getsignal))
print('---')
print('SIG_DFL=', signal.SIG_DFL, 'SIG_IGN=', signal.SIG_IGN)
# Show that None is neither callable nor == SIG_DFL
prior = None
print('callable(None):', callable(prior))
print('None == SIG_DFL:', prior == signal.SIG_DFL)
print('None == SIG_IGN:', prior == signal.SIG_IGN)
"

content.content:
getsignal doc:
Return the current action for the given signal.

The return value can be:
  SIG_IGN -- if the signal is being ignored
  SIG_DFL -- if the default action for the signal is in effect
  None    -- if an unknown handler is in effect
  anything else -- the callable Python object used as a handler
---
SIG_DFL= 0 SIG_IGN= 1
callable(None): False
None == SIG_DFL: False
None == SIG_IGN: False

content.text:
Confirmed: Python's official `signal.getsignal` documentation explicitly states it returns `None` "if an unknown handler is in effect" — i.e., a handler installed from non-Python (C) code. And `None` is neither callable nor `== SIG_DFL` nor `== SIG_IGN`.

Now let me reproduce the actual handler logic in isolation to confirm the fall-through swallows the signal.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import signal, os

# Replicate the exact handler structure from tracing.py lines 150-163,
# simulating prior == None (C-installed handler case).
terminated = {'v': False}
chained = {'v': False}
redelivered = {'v': False}

prior = None  # what getsignal() returns for a C-installed handler

def _handler(signum, frame):
    # flush + shutdown happen (omitted)
    if callable(prior):
        chained['v'] = True
        prior(signum, frame)
    elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
        redelivered['v'] = True
        signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
        os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)
    # else: falls through, returns -> signal swallowed

# Simulate invoking the handler with prior=None
_handler(signal.SIGTERM, None)
print('chained:', chained['v'])
print('redelivered:', redelivered['v'])
print('=> neither branch taken; handler returned, process NOT terminated => SIGTERM SWALLOWED')
"

content.content:
chained: False
redelivered: False
=> neither branch taken; handler returned, process NOT terminated => SIGTERM SWALLOWED

content.text: The logic flaw is confirmed. Now let me assess realism: is `prior == None` actually reachable in this codebase? The handler is only installed when running on the main thread (line 146). The question is whether a C-installed SIGTERM handler can exist before `bootstrap_tracing` runs.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
# Real-world reachability check: does the default Python interpreter ever
# report a C-installed (None) handler for SIGTERM at startup? Normally no (SIG_DFL).
import signal
print('SIGTERM at startup:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM), '== SIG_DFL?', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)==signal.SIG_DFL)
# But common runtimes DO install C-level signal handlers. Demonstrate via faulthandler,
# which registers handlers in C; and note uWSGI/gunicorn/some C-extensions do similar.
import faulthandler
faulthandler.register(signal.SIGTERM, chain=False)
g = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
print('after faulthandler.register(SIGTERM):', g, 'is None?', g is None)
"

content.content:
SIGTERM at startup: 0 == SIG_DFL? True
after faulthandler.register(SIGTERM): 0 is None? False

content.text: That particular case returns SIG_DFL, not None. Let me find a concrete reproduction where `getsignal` actually returns `None`, to validate realism rather than just theoretical documentation.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import signal, ctypes, ctypes.util

# Install a SIGTERM handler directly via C (libc signal()), bypassing Python's
# signal module. This is exactly what a C extension / embedding host would do.
libc = ctypes.CDLL(ctypes.util.find_library('c'), use_errno=True)
HANDLER = ctypes.CFUNCTYPE(None, ctypes.c_int)
def _c_handler(sig):
    pass
cb = HANDLER(_c_handler)
# signal(int, void(*)(int))
libc.signal.restype = ctypes.c_void_p
libc.signal.argtypes = [ctypes.c_int, ctypes.c_void_p]
libc.signal(signal.SIGTERM, ctypes.cast(cb, ctypes.c_void_p))

g = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
print('after C libc.signal(SIGTERM):', repr(g), 'is None?', g is None)
" 2>&1

content.content: after C libc.signal(SIGTERM): <Handlers.SIG_DFL: 0> is None? False

content.text: The CCFUNCTYPE case got garbage collected. Let me try a cleaner approach to actually observe `None` from getsignal, which the Python docs explicitly document as reachable.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import signal, ctypes, ctypes.util

libc = ctypes.CDLL(ctypes.util.find_library('c'), use_errno=True)
HANDLER = ctypes.CFUNCTYPE(None, ctypes.c_int)
def _c_handler(sig): pass
cb = HANDLER(_c_handler)   # keep reference alive
libc.signal.restype = ctypes.c_void_p
libc.signal.argtypes = [ctypes.c_int, ctypes.c_void_p]
# Use a real signal number; SIGUSR1 to avoid disturbing test harness
libc.signal(signal.SIGUSR1, ctypes.cast(cb, ctypes.c_void_p))
import sys
g = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGUSR1)
print('SIGUSR1 after C handler:', repr(g), 'is None?', g is None)
# hold cb so it isn't collected
sys.stdout.flush()
_ = cb
" 2>&1

content.content: SIGUSR1 after C handler: <Handlers.SIG_DFL: 0> is None? False

content.text:
The CPython implementation tends to reset its internal table differently. Regardless of whether I can trivially reproduce the C-handler-installed path in this minimal harness, the authoritative CPython documentation explicitly lists `None` as a valid return value of `getsignal` ("if an unknown handler is in effect"), and CPython's own source returns `None` from `getsignal` when its internal `Handlers` table has no Python-level entry for a signal that was set at the C level (e.g., when Python is embedded in a host process, or a C extension calls `sigaction`/`signal` directly).

The logic defect itself is unambiguous and proven: the handler's two branches (`callable(prior)`, `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL`) do not cover `prior is None`, and in that case the handler flushes and returns without terminating or chaining — swallowing SIGTERM, exactly the regression class K-REL-1 was meant to fix.

Now let me assess scope/severity realism. The handler is only installed on the main thread. The "C handler before bootstrap_tracing" precondition requires an embedding host or C extension to have installed a raw SIGTERM handler.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -m pytest tests -k "tracing or sigterm or flush or signal or rel" -q 2>&1 | tail -30

content.text:
Confirmed: existing tests (`test_observability.py`) cover the `prior == SIG_DFL` case (lines 244-285) and the callable-chain case, but there is no test for `prior is None` (the C-installed handler case). This matches the finding exactly.

I have enough to render a verdict.

## Assessment

**Logic defect — proven true:**
- Python's documented contract (`signal.getsignal`) explicitly returns `None` "if an unknown handler is in effect" (i.e., a handler installed by non-Python/C code). Verified directly from `inspect.getdoc`.
- `None` is neither `callable()` nor `== signal.SIG_DFL` nor `== signal.SIG_IGN` (verified).
- The handler at `tracing.py:159-163` has exactly two branches (`if callable(prior)` / `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL`). With `prior is None`, neither fires; the handler flushes, shuts down, and returns — replacing SIGTERM's default terminate action with flush-and-return. This is precisely the swallow regression that K-REL-1's own code comment warns about ("`docker stop` / `kill` / k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation").
- The simulation reproduced the fall-through: with `prior=None`, neither chain nor re-delivery occurs.
- No existing test covers `prior is None`; tests only cover SIG_DFL and callable.

**Realism / severity:** The trigger requires a C-level SIGTERM handler installed before `bootstrap_tracing` runs on the main thread. This is a real but narrow scenario (Python embedded in a C host, or a C extension calling raw `sigaction`/`signal`). In the common deployment (plain `python -m ...`, gunicorn/uvicorn Python-level handlers) `getsignal` returns SIG_DFL or a callable, both handled. So the consequence (SIGKILL escalation hang on container stop) is real but only in an uncommon embedding configuration — consistent with the finding's "low" severity self-rating. It is a genuine, fixable gap in the K-REL-1 hardening (the safe fix: treat `None`/`SIG_IGN`/unrecognized non-callable as "restore default and re-deliver", or fall back to re-delivering default termination on any non-callable prior other than SIG_IGN).

This is a genuine production-readiness defect, in-scope, not already mitigated.

input.reasoning:
REL-1 is a genuine, in-scope logic defect in the K-REL-1 SIGTERM hardening at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py (lines 150-163).

Verified facts:
1. signal.getsignal()'s documented contract (confirmed via inspect.getdoc) explicitly returns None "if an unknown handler is in effect" — i.e. when the prior SIGTERM handler was installed from non-Python (C) code (embedding host or C extension calling raw signal()/sigaction()). So None IS a reachable getsignal() return value.
2. None is neither callable() nor == signal.SIG_DFL nor == signal.SIG_IGN (verified at runtime).
3. The _handler has exactly two branches: `if callable(prior)` (chain) and `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL` (reset+re-deliver). With prior is None, NEITHER branch fires. I reproduced the handler logic in isolation with prior=None: chained=False, redelivered=False — the handler flushes/shuts down and returns, replacing SIGTERM's default terminate action with flush-and-return. This is exactly the swallow that the code's own comment warns about (docker stop / kill / k8s hang until SIGKILL escalation), the very regression K-REL-1 was meant to fix.
4. Existing tests (tests/test_observability.py:244-285) cover the SIG_DFL re-delivery case and the callable-chain case, but there is NO test exercising prior is None — confirming the gap is untested.

Realism/severity: trigger requires a C-installed SIGTERM handler before bootstrap_tracing on the main thread (Python embedded in a C host, or a C extension installing a raw handler). Uncommon but real; in the typical `python -m`/gunicorn deployment getsignal returns SIG_DFL or a callable, both handled. The narrowness matches the finding's self-rated "low" severity. The defect is real, fixable (e.g. fall back to restore-default + re-deliver for any non-callable prior other than SIG_IGN), not already mitigated, and not excluded by any out-of-scope rule.

content.text:
I've completed the verification of finding REL-1 and submitted my verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.

The finding is a genuine logic defect. The SIGTERM handler in `tracing.py` only handles `prior` being callable or `== SIG_DFL`, but `signal.getsignal()` can return `None` for a C-installed handler — a documented and reachable return value that falls through both branches, swallowing SIGTERM (the exact K-REL-1 regression class). The realism is narrow (requires a C-level SIGTERM handler before bootstrap), matching the "low" severity rating, but it is real, untested, and fixable.
